skill.name: impress
skill.description: Create, edit, and convert LibreOffice Impress presentations with ODP, PPTX, PDF, and template workflows.
skill.summary: Automates LibreOffice Impress presentation creation, conversion, and template-based slide workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly documentation examples for LibreOffice command-line use, Python subprocess calls, temporary directories, and reference links. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but the skill encourages executing external commands against presentation files, so publication should include a command-execution warning.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents soffice, unzip, zip, pip, and killall commands. These are legitimate for LibreOffice automation, but executing them on untrusted paths or files can modify local files, terminate processes, or process hostile documents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and are operational examples for the skill. They appear legitimate, but they still require user-controlled file paths and local process execution.

medium_findings.title: Template Archive Extraction and Repacking
medium_findings.description: The template generation example unzips a presentation template into a temporary directory, edits content.xml, and zips the result. This is expected for ODP automation, but malicious archives can exploit unsafe extraction behavior if copied into real workflows without validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess and temporary directory operations are explicitly shown in the template workflow. The example is not malicious, but archive handling has a clear security boundary.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URLs are links to LibreOffice documentation and the LibreOffice API reference. No evidence found of data exfiltration, remote code loading, or hidden network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible resource links to official LibreOffice documentation. They do not instruct the agent to send local data to an external service.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives for Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm and network reconnaissance alerts appear to match benign presentation terms, slide access, conversion examples, localhost UNO socket setup, and process cleanup. No evidence found of cryptography, port scanning, or reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these lines describe LibreOffice presentation operations and a localhost UNO endpoint. The suspicious labels do not match the semantic content.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run with Archive Utilities
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python example uses subprocess.run to call unzip and zip. This is useful for ODP files, but marketplace users should treat template paths and archives as untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact subprocess calls are present and process paths supplied to the helper function. The risk depends on how a downstream user adapts the example.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands for Document Conversion
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides shell commands for headless document conversion and process control. These commands should be run only with trusted files and explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands are legitimate LibreOffice workflows. They still execute local programs and can affect files or running LibreOffice processes.

content.value_statement: Presentation work often requires repetitive format conversion and template updates. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide LibreOffice Impress workflows for ODP, PPTX, PDF, and slide automation.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of new ODP presentations and reusable Impress templates.
Explains conversion workflows between ODP, PPTX, PDF, HTML, and related formats.
Provides command-line examples for headless LibreOffice conversion tasks.
Shows Python UNO patterns for creating and saving presentation documents.
Describes template-based slide generation by editing ODP content files.
limitations: Requires LibreOffice and related command-line tools to be installed locally.
Does not provide a packaged automation script or tested library wrapper.
Conversion quality depends on fonts, layout complexity, and LibreOffice compatibility.
Shell and Python examples need review before use with untrusted files.

use_cases.title: Batch Convert Presentation Files
use_cases.description: Convert many ODP decks into PDF or PPTX for sharing, archiving, or review workflows.

use_cases.title: Generate Recurring Slide Decks
use_cases.description: Use ODP templates and structured content to create repeatable reports or status presentations.

use_cases.title: Prototype Impress Automation
use_cases.description: Explore UNO scripting and command-line patterns before building a larger presentation automation pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the impress skill to show me how to convert presentation.odp to PDF with LibreOffice. Include the exact command and any checks I should run first.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Template Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the impress skill to design a workflow for generating a weekly status deck from an ODP template and a small content map.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Batch Export
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the impress skill to plan a safe batch conversion process for a folder of ODP files into PDF files. Include validation and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Build UNO Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the impress skill to outline a Python UNO approach for creating an Impress deck with title slides, charts, speaker notes, and PDF export.

output_examples.input: Convert one ODP presentation into a PDF for distribution.
output_examples.output: A concise LibreOffice command, a note to verify the output path, and checks for fonts and layout after export.

output_examples.input: Create a recurring project update deck from a template.
Troubleshoot a failed headless conversion job.
output_examples.output: A focused checklist covering LibreOffice availability, file permissions, stuck processes, input format support, and output directory access.

best_practices: Run conversion commands only on trusted files and known output directories.
Keep source ODP files and templates in version control for repeatable edits.
Review exported decks manually when fonts, animations, or layouts are important.
anti_patterns: Do not run shell commands from the skill against unknown presentation files without review.
Do not assume PPTX and ODP conversions preserve every animation or layout detail.
Do not overwrite original decks during automation without a backup or versioned source.

faq.question: What does this skill help automate?
faq.answer: It helps with LibreOffice Impress presentation creation, conversion, template generation, and basic slide automation workflows.

faq.question: Does it require LibreOffice?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows depend on LibreOffice commands or the LibreOffice UNO API.

faq.question: Can it convert PPTX files?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents conversions between PPTX, ODP, PDF, and other supported LibreOffice formats.

faq.question: Is it safe to run on untrusted files?
faq.answer: Use caution. Presentation files and archives should be treated as untrusted input before running local conversion or extraction commands.

faq.question: Does it include a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, command examples, and scripting patterns rather than a packaged application.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
